title: Roche cobas h 232 Blood Gas Analyzer - Test Cartridge Not Recognized
description: Cartridge not detected, invalid strip errors, or failure to initiate testing due to insertion, contamination, or cartridge condition issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while actively testing a patient sample. Use a backup analyzer if testing is needed immediately.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Insert a test cartridge and observe the analyzer response. Check for: “Strip not recognized” or similar message, no response after insertion, or repeated prompts to reinsert cartridge.

steps.title: Check Cartridge Type and Expiration (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Confirm correct test type for the analyzer, check expiration date on cartridge, and ensure cartridge packaging is intact and unopened prior to use.

steps.title: Inspect Cartridge Handling and Storage Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm cartridges were stored per manufacturer requirements, allow refrigerated cartridges to reach room temperature before use, and avoid using cartridges exposed to moisture or condensation.

steps.title: Check Cartridge Insertion Technique
steps.instructions: Insert cartridge fully until seated, ensure correct orientation, and remove and reinsert firmly.

steps.title: Inspect Cartridge Slot for Contamination or Damage
steps.instructions: Visually inspect slot for dirt, dried fluids, or debris, clean external accessible areas per manufacturer-approved methods, and do not insert tools into the slot.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Analyzer
steps.instructions: Power off the analyzer, wait 10–15 seconds, power back on, and retry with a known-good cartridge.

steps.title: Test with a Known-Good Cartridge Lot
steps.instructions: Use a cartridge from a different lot number if available.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer not recognizing test cartridges during patient testing.
CCR.Cause: Contamination found in cartridge slot preventing proper detection.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned cartridge interface and verified proper operation with known-good cartridge.

helpfulDetails: Cartridge type and expiration date verified
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Roche cobas h 232 Blood Gas Analyzer - QC Failure or Analyzer Lockout
description: QC failures, analyzer lockout, or inability to run patient tests due to control issues, strip problems, or configuration-related restrictions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while the analyzer is needed for active patient testing. Move testing to a backup device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported QC Failure or Lockout
steps.instructions: Power on the analyzer and review the screen. Confirm QC failure message or lockout notification, note any specific error codes or prompts, and attempt to access testing mode.

steps.title: Check QC Material Validity (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Verify QC material is not expired, confirm proper storage conditions, and mix or prepare QC material per protocol.

steps.title: Verify Test Strip and Code Chip Compatibility
steps.instructions: Confirm correct test strips are being used, ensure code chip matches strip lot number, and reseat or replace code chip if applicable.

steps.title: Inspect Test Strip Storage and Handling
steps.instructions: Check that strips are stored in sealed containers, ensure strips are within expiration date, and avoid humidity or temperature extremes.

steps.title: Check Analyzer Temperature Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm device is within operating temperature range and allow analyzer to acclimate if moved from a different environment.

steps.title: Clean Test Strip Port and Contacts
steps.instructions: Inspect strip port for debris or residue, clean externally per manufacturer-approved method, and do not insert foreign objects into the port.

steps.title: Restart the Analyzer
steps.instructions: Power the unit off, remove battery briefly if applicable, restart, and repeat QC process.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer locked out due to repeated QC failure and unable to run patient tests.
CCR.Cause: Expired QC material and mismatched code chip with test strip lot.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced QC material, installed correct code chip, and successfully passed QC; returned unit to service.

helpfulDetails: QC lot numbers and expiration dates verified
Test strip condition and storage checked
Code chip matched to strip lot
Device successfully passed QC or removed from service

title: Roche cobas h 232 Blood Gas Analyzer - Wireless or LIS Connectivity Failure
description: Troubleshooting failed result transmission and LIS connectivity issues caused by docking, network, or configuration problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while the analyzer is needed for active patient testing. Use a backup device if results are urgently required.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Connectivity Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to transmit results or dock the analyzer and observe for missing LIS/EMR results, no communication indicator, or transmission errors.

steps.title: Check Docking Station Function (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Ensure analyzer is fully seated in the dock, check for docking indicators, and reseat the device.

steps.title: Verify Dock Power and Network Connection
steps.instructions: Confirm dock power, ensure Ethernet cable is connected, and test the network port if possible.

steps.title: Inspect Network Cable and Ports
steps.instructions: Check for cable damage, swap with a known-good cable, and try a different port if available.

steps.title: Confirm Analyzer Communication Settings
steps.instructions: Verify correct communication mode, configuration settings, and LIS/middleware registration.

steps.title: Check for Middleware or LIS Downtime
steps.instructions: Confirm system status with IT or lab staff and check if other devices are affected.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Analyzer and Dock
steps.instructions: Restart both the analyzer and docking station to clear temporary communication faults.

steps.title: Test with Known-Good Dock or Location
steps.instructions: Use a working dock or alternate network location to isolate the issue source.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer not transmitting results to LIS when docked.
CCR.Cause: Faulty Ethernet cable at docking station preventing network communication.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced network cable and verified successful result transmission to LIS.

title: Roche cobas h 232 Blood Gas Analyzer - Battery or Power Failure
description: Analyzer not powering on, shutting down unexpectedly, or failing to charge due to battery, charger, or external power issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while the analyzer is needed for active patient testing. Move testing to a backup device.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Power Issue
steps.instructions: Attempt to power on the analyzer using the normal power button and observe for no power, brief startup then shutdown, or charging indicator issues.

steps.title: Check Battery Charge Level (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Place the analyzer on the charging dock, observe for charging indicator, allow several minutes, and attempt power-on again.

steps.title: Verify Charger/Docking Station Power
steps.instructions: Confirm outlet is working, check power adapter connections, and look for power indicator lights on the dock.

steps.title: Reseat Analyzer in Dock Properly
steps.instructions: Remove and reseat analyzer, ensure proper alignment, and check for debris on contacts.

steps.title: Inspect Charging Contacts
steps.instructions: Inspect analyzer and dock contacts for dirt, corrosion, or damage and clean gently if needed.

steps.title: Test with Known-Good Charger or Dock
steps.instructions: Place analyzer on a known-good dock or charger and attempt to charge and power on.

steps.title: Observe for Abnormal Behavior
steps.instructions: Check for excessive heat, no charging indication, or shutdown while docked.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer would not power on and showed no charging indication.
CCR.Cause: Failed internal battery—unit would not charge on multiple known-good docks.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent for repair; verified charger functionality.

helpfulDetails: Any heat, smell, or abnormal shutdown behavior

title: Roche cobas h 232 Blood Gas Analyzer - Display or Keypad Errors
description: Troubleshooting guide for unresponsive keypad, incorrect display, or screen issues caused by power instability, contamination, or external damage.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot while the analyzer is needed for active patient testing. Move testing to another analyzer.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Power on the device and attempt normal operation. Observe for blank or dim display, frozen or distorted screen, and keypad not responding or delayed input.

steps.title: Check Battery Status (Most Common Cause)
steps.instructions: Check battery level indicator, replace with a fully charged or known-good battery, and power cycle the device.

steps.title: Inspect for Contamination or Physical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect keypad surface for debris or dried fluids, clean using approved disinfectant wipes, and ensure no liquid has entered seams or edges.

steps.title: Check for Physical Damage
steps.instructions: Inspect screen for cracks, discoloration, or dead areas and check for loose housing or misaligned buttons.

steps.title: Perform a Full Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Power off the device, remove the battery, wait 30 seconds, reinstall battery, and power on.

steps.title: Verify Operating Environment
steps.instructions: Ensure device is within recommended temperature range and avoid direct bright light or glare on the screen.

CCR.Complaint: Analyzer display frozen and keypad not responding during use.
CCR.Cause: Failed display/keypad interface after ruling out battery and contamination.
CCR.Resolution: Removed from service and sent to vendor for repair.

helpfulDetails: Battery level verified or battery swapped
Evidence of contamination or fluid exposure

title: Roche Accu-Chek Inform II Blood Glucose Meter - Strip Not Recognized or Insertion Errors
description: Meter fails to detect test strips, shows insertion errors, or does not start testing despite proper strip placement.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the meter from patient use before troubleshooting. Use an alternate device for testing.

steps.title: Verify Correct Test Strips
steps.instructions: Confirm the strips are Accu-Chek Inform II compatible and not from another system. Check strip vial labeling and confirm hospital-approved supply.

steps.title: Check Strip Expiration and Storage Conditions
steps.instructions: Inspect the expiration date and ensure strips were stored properly (sealed vial, controlled environment). Open a new vial if uncertain and ensure lid was tightly closed.

steps.title: Inspect Strip Insertion Technique
steps.instructions: Ensure the strip is inserted correctly and fully seated. Insert strip firmly until it stops and confirm correct orientation (contacts first, correct side up).

steps.title: Inspect Strip Port for Contamination or Damage
steps.instructions: Check the meter’s strip port for debris, dried fluid, or physical damage. Use a flashlight to inspect port and clean externally per approved cleaning procedure (no internal probing).

steps.title: Test with Known Good Strip and Second Meter
steps.instructions: Use a strip from a confirmed good vial and compare behavior on another meter. Insert same strip type into another working device.

steps.title: Check for Software Prompts or Lockouts
steps.instructions: Observe if the device is displaying QC requirements or lockout messages. Review screen messages before and after insertion.

steps.title: Restart the Device
steps.instructions: Power cycle the meter to reset detection logic. Turn device off and back on and reattempt strip insertion.

CCR.Complaint: Meter not recognizing test strips; unable to perform glucose testing.
CCR.Cause: Contamination found in strip port causing poor contact with test strips.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned strip port externally and verified proper operation with new test strips.

helpfulDetails: Strip lot and expiration date checked
Final device status (returned to service or sent for repair)

title: Roche Accu-Chek Inform II Blood Glucose Meter - QC Lockout or Control Failures
description: Meter locked out due to failed or overdue QC, control solution errors, or improper QC procedure preventing patient testing.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not perform patient testing while the meter is in QC lockout. Move testing to another verified meter. Expected: Patient testing continues safely on another device. Why it matters: QC lockout prevents use of a device that may produce inaccurate results.

steps.title: Verify the Lockout Message
steps.instructions: Power on the meter and confirm the exact message (e.g., QC required, QC failed, or lockout). Expected: Clear indication of QC-related lockout. Why it matters: Confirms the issue is QC-related, not a hardware or strip error.

steps.title: Check QC Status and Requirements
steps.instructions: Review the QC status in the meter menu. Determine if QC is overdue. Confirm if the correct level (Level 1 or Level 2) is required. Expected: Meter indicates which QC level is needed. Why it matters: Performing the wrong level or skipping required QC will keep the device locked.

steps.title: Verify Control Solution Integrity
steps.instructions: Check that the control solution is not expired. Confirm proper labeling (Level 1 or Level 2). Ensure it was stored correctly and not overheated or contaminated. Expected: Control solution is valid and usable. Why it matters: Expired or compromised solution is a common cause of QC failure.

steps.title: Inspect Test Strips
steps.instructions: Check strip vial is not expired. Verify strips are correct for Accu-Chek Inform II. Ensure vial is properly closed and stored. Expected: Strips are valid and undamaged. Why it matters: Bad strips can cause repeated QC failures.

steps.title: Perform QC Test Properly
steps.instructions: Insert strip fully. Apply control solution correctly (do not smear or underfill). Ensure adequate sample volume. Expected: QC result falls within acceptable range. Why it matters: Technique errors are a frequent cause of QC failure.

steps.title: Check Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: Ensure room temperature is within operating range. Avoid excessive humidity or cold exposure. Expected: Stable testing environment. Why it matters: Environmental factors can affect strip and control performance.

steps.title: Repeat QC with New Supplies
steps.instructions: Open a new vial of strips. Use a new control solution bottle. Expected: QC passes with fresh materials. Why it matters: Confirms issue was supply-related, not device-related.

steps.title: Check Docking / Connectivity (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: Ensure the meter has synced recently. Place in dock or confirm wireless connection. Verify time/date are correct. Expected: Meter is up to date with system requirements. Why it matters: Some lockouts are enforced by connectivity or outdated QC records.

steps.title: Reboot the Meter
steps.instructions: Power cycle the device and retry QC. Expected: Meter restarts cleanly and allows QC attempt. Why it matters: Clears minor software or interface issues.

CCR.Complaint: Meter locked out and unable to perform patient testing due to QC failure.
CCR.Cause: Expired control solution causing QC results outside acceptable range.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced control solution and strips, performed successful QC, and returned meter to service.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (passed QC or removed from service)

title: Roche Accu-Chek Inform II Blood Glucose Meter - Connectivity or Data Upload Failure
description: Failure to upload patient results, docking station communication issues, or wireless connectivity problems preventing data transfer to the hospital system.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not delay patient care while troubleshooting connectivity. Use another validated meter if results cannot be uploaded.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm symptoms with staff including results not uploading, dock not syncing, or wireless icon missing or error displayed.

steps.title: Check Docking Station Connection (Most Common Issue)
steps.instructions: Ensure meter is fully seated, dock has power, and Ethernet cable is securely connected.

steps.title: Test Known-Good Dock
steps.instructions: Place the meter in a different working dock and attempt upload to determine if issue is dock-related.

steps.title: Check Network Availability
steps.instructions: Verify network port activity, confirm other devices function, and check for outages.

steps.title: Inspect Meter Contacts and Dock Pins
steps.instructions: Check for debris, bent pins, or contamination and clean per protocol.

steps.title: Reboot the Meter
steps.instructions: Power off and restart the meter, then re-dock and retry upload.

steps.title: Verify User Login and Device Status
steps.instructions: Ensure operator is logged in and device is not in QC lockout or restricted mode.

steps.title: Check Wireless Function (If Applicable)
steps.instructions: Confirm wireless is enabled, signal is present, and test in known coverage area.

steps.title: Attempt Manual Sync or Data Transfer
steps.instructions: Trigger manual upload if available and observe for errors.

CCR.Complaint: Accu-Chek Inform II not uploading patient results when docked.
CCR.Cause: Faulty docking station with no network connectivity.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced dock and verified successful data upload.

title: Roche Accu-Chek Inform II Blood Glucose Meter - Battery Not Charging or Docking Issues
description: Meter not charging in base, low battery warnings, or failure to power when docked due to external power or docking issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the meter from clinical use if battery is critically low or unreliable. Use another meter for patient testing.

steps.title: Verify Docking Behavior
steps.instructions: Place the meter firmly into the docking station. Observe for charging indicators (LEDs or on-screen battery icon).

steps.title: Check Power to Docking Station
steps.instructions: Ensure the dock is plugged into a known working outlet. Verify power LED (if present) is illuminated. Test outlet with another device. Check power adapter connection.

steps.title: Inspect Docking Contacts (Meter and Base)
steps.instructions: Visually inspect metal charging contacts on both the meter and dock. Look for dirt, corrosion, or damage. Clean gently with approved wipe if needed.

steps.title: Reseat the Meter in Dock
steps.instructions: Remove and reinsert the meter slowly and firmly. Ensure proper alignment.

steps.title: Try a Known Working Dock
steps.instructions: Place the meter into another verified working docking station.

steps.title: Check Battery Condition
steps.instructions: If the meter powers on briefly but does not hold charge: observe for rapid battery drain and check battery age if known.

steps.title: Inspect Dock Cable and Connections
steps.instructions: Check for loose or damaged cables, frayed power cords, and poor connection at power brick.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Meter
steps.instructions: Remove the meter from the dock and restart it if possible, then reseat it in the dock.

CCR.Complaint: Meter not charging in docking station and showing low battery warning.
CCR.Cause: Faulty docking station power supply preventing charging.
CCR.Resolution: Replaced docking station and verified proper charging function.

helpfulDetails: Final device status (working / removed from service)

title: Roche Accu-Chek Inform II Blood Glucose Meter - Touchscreen or Keypad Not Responding
description: Unresponsive touchscreen or keypad inputs, delayed response, or inability to navigate menus due to external, environmental, or accessory-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Remove the device from patient use and obtain a functioning meter before troubleshooting. Expected: No delay in patient testing Why it matters: Prevents interruption in glucose monitoring and patient care

steps.title: Inspect Screen for Contamination or Damage
steps.instructions: Check the touchscreen for dirt, dried solution, glucose residue, or cracks. Clean screen using approved disinfectant wipe and dry thoroughly. Expected: Screen is clean and free of residue Why it matters: Contamination can block touch sensitivity or cause false inputs

steps.title: Check for Glove or User Interaction Issues
steps.instructions: Verify the type of gloves being used (if applicable). Try operating with clean, dry gloves or bare finger (per facility policy). Expected: Device responds normally Why it matters: Some gloves or moisture interfere with capacitive touch response

steps.title: Restart the Device (Soft Reset)
steps.instructions: Power cycle the meter. Hold power button until device shuts down, restart and test responsiveness. Expected: Screen responds after reboot Why it matters: Temporary software freezes are common and often resolved with reboot

steps.title: Check Battery Charge and Docking Status
steps.instructions: Low power can affect screen responsiveness. Verify battery level and place meter in dock and confirm charging indicator. Expected: Device has adequate charge Why it matters: Low voltage conditions can cause erratic behavior

steps.title: Inspect Docking Station and Contacts
steps.instructions: Poor docking connections may prevent proper charging or communication. Check docking pins for damage or contamination and try a known-good dock. Expected: Device charges reliably Why it matters: Charging issues can lead to intermittent functionality

steps.title: Observe for Screen Lag or Partial Response
steps.instructions: Determine if the issue is complete failure or partial responsiveness. Attempt multiple areas of the screen and note if certain areas respond while others do not. Expected: Uniform response across screen Why it matters: Partial response may indicate digitizer failure rather than software issue

steps.title: Check for Software Lock or Login Screen Freeze
steps.instructions: Ensure the device is not stuck on a login or QC lock screen. Verify prompts on screen and attempt navigation or login. Expected: Device transitions between screens Why it matters: Perceived unresponsiveness may be due to workflow restrictions

steps.title: Swap with a Known Good Device
steps.instructions: Compare behavior with another meter. Expected: Known-good device functions normally Why it matters: Confirms issue is device-specific and not environmental

CCR.Complaint: Touchscreen not responding to user input during patient testing
CCR.Cause: Touchscreen unresponsive after cleaning and reboot; likely internal digitizer failure
CCR.Resolution: Removed device from service and sent to vendor for repair evaluation

helpfulDetails: Any visible cracks or fluid exposure

title: Roche Urisys 1100 Urine Analyzer - Strip Tray Movement Error
description: Troubleshooting Urisys 1100 strip tray movement errors caused by tray obstruction, improper insertion, contamination, strip issues, or external handling problems.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Urisys 1100 is not actively being used to report a patient urine result. If patient testing is needed immediately, have staff use another validated analyzer or follow the laboratory’s approved backup procedure. Expected outcome: No patient result is delayed or released from a device with an unresolved tray movement problem.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error
steps.instructions: Ask staff when the strip tray movement error occurs. Check whether it happens: At startup; When the tray extends; When the tray retracts; After a strip is placed; During the read cycle; Intermittently after cleaning or heavy use. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is understood before assuming a mechanical fault.

steps.title: Stop Testing and Remove the Strip
steps.instructions: Remove any reagent strip from the tray if present. Confirm with lab staff whether the sample needs to be repeated according to policy. Expected outcome: No incomplete or questionable result is used.

steps.title: Check the Strip Tray for Proper Seating
steps.instructions: Inspect the strip tray and confirm it is fully inserted and aligned correctly. Gently remove and reinstall the tray if it appears partially seated, crooked, or not moving freely. Expected outcome: The tray sits squarely and can move without obvious resistance. If reseating the tray resolves the error, return the analyzer to service only after a successful function check or QC per lab policy.

steps.title: Inspect for Physical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Look for dried urine, reagent strip fragments, paper, labels, tape, debris, or crystallized residue around the strip tray path. Do not force the tray if it is stuck. Expected outcome: The tray path is clear of visible obstruction.

steps.title: Check for Contamination on the Tray
steps.instructions: Inspect the strip tray surface and surrounding accessible area for dried sample, reagent buildup, sticky residue, or cleaning solution deposits. Clean only the user-accessible tray area using facility-approved cleaning materials and Roche-compatible cleaning guidance. Expected outcome: The tray is clean, dry, and able to move normally.

steps.title: Verify the Correct Reagent Strip Is Being Used
steps.instructions: Confirm staff are using the correct Roche-compatible urine test strips for the Urisys 1100. Check that strips are not expired, curled, wet, swollen, or damaged. Expected outcome: The strip fits correctly on the tray and does not interfere with tray travel.

steps.title: Check Strip Placement Technique
steps.instructions: Have staff demonstrate how the strip is placed on the tray. Verify the strip is positioned flat, correctly oriented, and not hanging over the edge of the tray. Expected outcome: The strip remains aligned during tray movement. Why it matters: Misplaced or curled strips can drag, shift, or block tray motion.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Analyzer
steps.instructions: Turn the analyzer off using the normal power switch. Wait briefly, then power it back on and allow the startup sequence to complete. Expected outcome: The tray initializes without error. If the tray movement error clears, perform QC or a facility-approved verification before returning the device to routine use.

steps.title: Check the Power Supply and Outlet
steps.instructions: Confirm the power adapter is firmly connected to the analyzer and wall outlet. Test the outlet or move the analyzer to a known-good outlet if power behavior appears unstable. Expected outcome: The analyzer receives stable power during tray movement.

steps.title: Observe Tray Movement Without a Strip
steps.instructions: Run the appropriate startup or test preparation sequence without placing a patient strip, if allowed by lab procedure. Watch for hesitation, grinding, clicking, uneven movement, or failure to fully extend or retract. Expected outcome: The tray moves smoothly through its expected travel path.

steps.title: Check for Recent Cleaning, Spill, or Drop History
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether the analyzer was recently cleaned, exposed to a spill, moved, dropped, or had the tray removed. Expected outcome: Any handling-related cause is identified before escalation.

steps.title: Do Not Force the Tray
steps.instructions: If the tray is stuck, binding, or making abnormal mechanical noise, do not pull or push it by force. Expected outcome: Further damage to the tray drive mechanism is avoided.

steps.title: Repeat QC After Corrective Action
steps.instructions: Once the tray moves normally, have the laboratory perform required QC or verification before patient testing resumes. Expected outcome: Analyzer function is confirmed before clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported that the Roche Urisys 1100 displayed a strip tray movement error during urine strip testing.
CCR.Cause: Strip tray was found partially seated with dried residue in the accessible tray path, preventing smooth tray travel.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the analyzer from active testing, cleaned and reseated the strip tray, power cycled the unit, and returned it to service after successful QC verification by lab staff.

helpfulDetails: Whether the error occurred at startup or during testing
Whether the strip tray extended, retracted, or stopped mid-cycle
Tray path inspected for debris or dried urine
Any grinding, clicking, binding, or unusual sound noted
Whether the analyzer was recently cleaned, moved, spilled on, or dropped

title: Roche Urisys 1100 Urine Analyzer - Strip Positioning / Timing Error
description: Troubleshooting Urisys 1100 strip placement, timing, tray movement, and reading errors caused by external or user-correctable issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer is not actively processing a patient specimen. If testing is urgent, have laboratory staff use another validated urine analyzer or follow the approved downtime procedure. Expected outcome: Patient testing is not delayed or reported from a device with unresolved strip positioning or timing concerns.

steps.title: Confirm the Reported Error
steps.instructions: Ask the operator what happened during testing. Check whether the issue involved: Strip placed too early or too late; Strip not sitting correctly on the tray; Analyzer not detecting the strip; Tray movement error; Timing error after dipping the strip; Read failure after strip transport; Intermittent errors with certain users or strip lots. Expected outcome: The failure is confirmed as a strip positioning, timing, or tray workflow issue.

steps.title: Stop Additional Patient Testing
steps.instructions: Do not allow continued patient testing until proper strip movement and reading are verified. Expected outcome: No questionable urine results are produced while the analyzer may be misreading strip position or timing.

steps.title: Check Strip Placement Technique
steps.instructions: Observe the operator placing a strip on the Urisys 1100 strip tray. Verify the strip is seated flat, aligned correctly, and positioned in the correct direction. Expected outcome: The strip sits properly in the tray without overhang, tilt, or pad misalignment. If correcting strip placement resolves the error, document the finding and return the device to service after successful verification.

steps.title: Verify Proper Timing After Dipping the Strip
steps.instructions: Confirm the operator is following the correct workflow after dipping and blotting the reagent strip. The strip should not sit too long before placement, and it should not be rushed into the tray before excess urine is removed. Expected outcome: The analyzer receives the strip within the expected test workflow window. If timing technique was the cause, retrain the user and stop troubleshooting once successful test operation is confirmed.

steps.title: Inspect the Strip Tray for Contamination
steps.instructions: Remove the analyzer from use and inspect the strip tray area for urine residue, dried reagent, crystallization, debris, or staining. Clean only the external/user-accessible tray surfaces according to facility procedure and manufacturer-approved cleaning guidance. Expected outcome: The tray area is clean and free of residue that could interfere with strip positioning or movement.

steps.title: Check for Mechanical Obstruction
steps.instructions: Inspect the tray path for anything preventing smooth movement, such as a loose strip, dried fluid, paper debris, or foreign material. Do not force the tray or disassemble the analyzer. Expected outcome: The tray path is clear and the tray can move normally through its expected travel.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Reagent Strip Type
steps.instructions: Verify the urine reagent strips are compatible with the Roche Urisys 1100 configuration. Confirm the strip bottle is not expired and was stored properly with the cap closed. Expected outcome: Strip type, lot, expiration, and storage condition are acceptable.

steps.title: Check for Damaged or Wet Strips
steps.instructions: Inspect several strips from the bottle. Look for curled strips, swollen pads, discoloration, moisture exposure, or reagent pad lifting. Expected outcome: The strip is flat, dry before use, and physically suitable for tray placement and reading.

steps.title: Try a Fresh Strip Bottle or Known-Good Lot
steps.instructions: If available, have the lab test using a fresh bottle or known-good lot following normal QC procedure. Expected outcome: If the error stops with a different bottle or lot, the original strips may be damaged, expired, contaminated, or poorly stored.

steps.title: Verify Analyzer Is on a Stable Surface
steps.instructions: Confirm the Urisys 1100 is placed on a level, stable bench and is not near vibration, heavy traffic, or a wet work surface. Expected outcome: Tray movement and strip positioning are not affected by bench instability or external disturbance.

steps.title: Check Power Stability
steps.instructions: Verify the power supply is fully seated at the analyzer and wall outlet. Confirm the outlet is live and not loose, switched off, or shared with equipment causing intermittent power drops. Expected outcome: The analyzer remains powered and stable throughout startup and testing.

steps.title: Restart the Analyzer
steps.instructions: Power the analyzer off, wait briefly, and restart it according to normal user-level procedure. Allow the unit to complete startup before attempting another test or QC run. Expected outcome: Temporary workflow or startup faults are cleared.

steps.title: Run QC or a Verification Test Per Lab Policy
steps.instructions: After cleaning, strip verification, and restart, have laboratory staff run the required QC or verification process. Expected outcome: The strip is accepted, positioned correctly, timed correctly, and read without error. If verification passes, return the analyzer to service and document the corrective action.

steps.title: Check for Repeat Failure
steps.instructions: If the strip positioning or timing error returns after cleaning, correct strip use, known-good strips, stable power, and proper operator technique, suspect a tray drive, strip detection, sensor, or internal timing issue. Expected outcome: External causes have been ruled out before escalation.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported the Roche Urisys 1100 was giving strip positioning errors during urine strip testing.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found dried urine residue on the strip tray area and inconsistent strip seating during operator placement.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned the accessible tray area, verified correct strip placement technique, ran QC successfully, and returned the analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue occurred during startup or active testing
Whether the strip tray moved normally
Strip lot number and expiration date
Whether strips appeared curled, wet, or discolored
Whether a known-good strip bottle was tested
Whether tray area had residue or debris

title: Roche Urisys 1100 Urine Analyzer - Optics / Photometer Read Failure
description: Troubleshooting Urisys 1100 optical, strip reading, photometer, or reflectance failures caused by external or user-correctable conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Urisys 1100 is not being used to process or report active patient urine results. If patient testing is needed, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow the approved downtime/manual testing process. Expected outcome: Patient results are not reported from an analyzer with unresolved optical or photometer read concerns.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Failure
steps.instructions: Ask laboratory staff when the read failure occurs. Check whether it happens: During startup; During calibration or self-check; When reading a urine strip; With every strip; With one strip lot; Intermittently after cleaning or maintenance. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is understood before optics or internal hardware are suspected.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Contamination Around the Strip Area
steps.instructions: Inspect the strip tray, strip guide, and reading area for urine residue, dried control material, strip debris, dust, or cleaning residue. Clean only external and user-accessible areas using the facility-approved method and Roche-approved cleaning guidance. Expected outcome: The strip path and optical read area are free of visible contamination. If cleaning resolves the error, return the analyzer to service only after successful QC and document the work order. Stop here.

steps.title: Verify Correct Strip Type
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer is being used with the correct Roche-compatible urine test strips for the Urisys 1100 configuration. Make sure staff are not using an incorrect strip type, expired strips, or strips from a compromised bottle. Expected outcome: The analyzer is reading the correct strip format and chemistry pad layout.

steps.title: Check Strip Storage and Handling
steps.instructions: Verify the strip bottle cap is closed, strips are dry, and the bottle has not been exposed to moisture, heat, light, or contamination. Ask whether the issue began after opening a new strip bottle or changing lots. Expected outcome: Strip quality is ruled out as the cause of abnormal reflectance or photometer read failure.

steps.title: Run a Known-Good Strip or QC Check
steps.instructions: Have laboratory staff run QC or an approved check using a known-good strip lot and proper workflow. Observe whether the analyzer reads normally, rejects the strip, or produces the same optical/photometer error. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as analyzer-related or narrowed to strip/QC material handling.

steps.title: Inspect Strip Placement and Timing
steps.instructions: Confirm the strip is being placed correctly on the tray and inserted according to the normal analyzer workflow. A misaligned strip, delayed placement, excess sample, or poor blotting can interfere with the photometer reading. Expected outcome: The strip is positioned consistently and reaches the read area correctly.

steps.title: Check for Excess Sample or Carryover
steps.instructions: Ask staff to confirm strips are blotted properly and not dripping urine or control material into the tray area. Look for pooling, wet surfaces, or residue that could affect optical reflection. Expected outcome: The analyzer is not being affected by excess fluid, residue, or repeated contamination.

steps.title: Verify Ambient Conditions
steps.instructions: Confirm the analyzer is on a stable, level surface away from direct sunlight, strong glare, excessive vibration, heat, or condensation. Optical urine analyzers can be affected by environmental conditions that interfere with consistent reflectance readings. Expected outcome: The analyzer is operating in a suitable location.

steps.title: Power Cycle the Analyzer Safely
steps.instructions: Confirm no testing is in progress. Power the analyzer off, wait briefly, and restart it. Observe whether startup completes normally or the same photometer/read error returns. Expected outcome: Temporary software or startup state issues are cleared. If the analyzer completes startup and passes QC afterward, document the issue and return it to service. Stop here.

steps.title: Check Power Source and Adapter
steps.instructions: Verify the power adapter is fully seated and connected to a known-good outlet. Check for loose power connections, damaged cords, flickering display behavior, or unexpected resets. Expected outcome: Unstable power is ruled out as a contributor to read failures.

steps.title: Review Error Behavior After Cleaning and QC
steps.instructions: If the read failure continues after verifying strips, cleaning, environment, operator workflow, and power, do not continue repeated patient or QC testing. Expected outcome: Common external causes have been ruled out without unnecessary internal disassembly.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported the Roche Urisys 1100 was producing photometer read errors during urine strip testing.
CCR.Cause: External troubleshooting found dried urine residue on the strip tray and reading path, with QC failing before cleaning.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned user-accessible strip tray area, verified correct Roche-compatible strips, repeated QC successfully, and returned analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether failure occurs at startup or during strip reading
Strip lot and expiration date checked
QC lot and expiration date checked
Analyzer location checked for glare or vibration

title: Roche Urisys 1100 Urine Analyzer - Printer / Paper Feed Issue
description: Troubleshooting Urisys 1100 printer, paper feed, blank printout, poor print quality, or report output issues caused by external/user-accessible conditions.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Urisys 1100 is not actively processing a patient urine test before troubleshooting the printer or opening the paper compartment. If results are needed immediately, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow approved downtime documentation procedures. Expected outcome: Patient testing and result reporting are not delayed or compromised by printer troubleshooting.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Printer Issue
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed. Common reports may include: No printout after testing; Paper not advancing; Partial report; Blank report; Faded or unreadable print; Paper jam; Printer noise with no paper movement; Results visible on screen but not printed. Expected outcome: The issue is confirmed as a printer or paper feed problem rather than a testing, LIS, operator workflow, or result storage issue.

steps.title: Confirm Results Are Still Available
steps.instructions: Check whether the analyzer displays or stores the result after the test. Do not assume the test failed just because the printout failed. Expected outcome: Result availability is confirmed before any repeat testing is considered.

steps.title: Check Printer Paper Supply
steps.instructions: Open the printer paper compartment and verify that paper is loaded. Confirm the roll is not empty, loose, crushed, wet, contaminated, or installed incorrectly. Expected outcome: The analyzer has usable paper installed and the roll can rotate freely. If replacing or reseating the paper restores printing, stop troubleshooting and document the correction.

steps.title: Verify Correct Paper Orientation
steps.instructions: Confirm the paper is installed in the correct feed direction for the Urisys 1100 printer. Thermal printer paper will only print properly on the thermal-coated side. Expected outcome: Paper feeds correctly and printed text appears normally. If the report was blank and reversing/reloading the roll restores print output, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect for Paper Jam or Torn Paper
steps.instructions: Look for small pieces of torn paper, folded paper, adhesive residue, or debris in the paper path. Remove only visible, accessible obstruction without forcing the printer mechanism. Expected outcome: The paper path is clear and the paper can advance normally.

steps.title: Check the Printer Cover or Paper Door
steps.instructions: Confirm the printer cover is fully closed and seated correctly. Some small analyzers may not feed or print properly if the cover is not seated. Expected outcome: The printer cover is secure and no paper compartment error is present.

steps.title: Test Paper Feed Function
steps.instructions: Use the analyzer’s normal paper feed or print function, if available, to confirm whether the printer advances paper. Expected outcome: Paper advances smoothly without slipping, grinding, bunching, or stalling. If the paper advances normally and reports print clearly, return the device to service.

steps.title: Check for Faded or Poor Print Quality
steps.instructions: If the printer feeds paper but the report is faint, patchy, or blank, verify that approved thermal paper is being used. Replace the paper roll with a known-good roll if available. Expected outcome: Print quality improves with correct, fresh thermal paper.

steps.title: Inspect for Contamination Near the Paper Path
steps.instructions: Check for urine residue, disinfectant buildup, dust, paper fibers, or dried contamination around the printer opening and paper path. Clean only external and user-accessible areas using facility-approved cleaning methods. Expected outcome: Paper feeds without drag, sticking, or contamination.

steps.title: Check Power Stability
steps.instructions: Verify the Urisys 1100 is connected to a known-good outlet or approved power source. Confirm the analyzer powers on normally and does not restart, dim, freeze, or shut down during printing. Expected outcome: Printer failure is not being caused by unstable power.

steps.title: Run a Test Print or Control Workflow
steps.instructions: Perform a non-patient print verification, such as a test print, QC result print, or previous result print if allowed by facility policy. Expected outcome: The printer produces a complete, readable report. If the issue is resolved, return the analyzer to service after confirming normal operation.

steps.title: Do Not Force the Printer Mechanism
steps.instructions: If the printer motor runs but paper does not advance, or if the roller appears stuck, do not pry, force, or disassemble the internal printer mechanism. Expected outcome: The device is protected from additional damage and the problem is escalated appropriately.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported that the Roche Urisys 1100 would not print patient reports and the paper was not feeding after testing.
CCR.Cause: Found the thermal paper roll installed incorrectly, causing blank output and poor paper advancement.
CCR.Resolution: Reloaded the paper roll in the correct orientation, verified paper feed, completed a test print, and returned the analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Alarm behavior or printer message observed
Paper roll, cover, or printer accessories checked or swapped
Power behavior, outlet, or power source tested
Environmental factors such as moisture, contamination, dust, paper fibers, or disinfectant buildup near the paper path
Indicator lights, screen messages, blank output, faded output, partial print, unusual printer noise, or no-print condition noted
Final device status: returned to service or removed from service

title: Roche Urisys 1100 Urine Analyzer - QC Failure / Strip Lot Setup Issue
description: Troubleshooting QC failure, strip lot setup errors, calibration strip issues, or abnormal urine strip recovery on the Roche Urisys 1100.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions:
Confirm the Urisys 1100 is not being used to report active patient results while QC is failing or strip lot setup is questionable.

If patient testing is needed, have staff use another validated analyzer or follow approved downtime/manual testing procedures.

Expected outcome: No patient results are released from an analyzer with unresolved QC or strip setup concerns.

steps.title: Verify the Reported QC or Lot Setup Issue
steps.instructions:
Ask laboratory staff what specifically failed.

Check whether the issue involves:

QC result outside acceptable range
New strip lot not accepted
Calibration strip setup failure
Unexpected positive or negative chemistry recovery
Repeated QC failure on one level
Repeated QC failure across multiple pads
Failure only after opening a new strip bottle or changing lot

Expected outcome: The issue is identified as QC recovery, lot setup, calibration, or operator workflow before troubleshooting continues.

steps.title: Remove the Analyzer From Active Testing
steps.instructions:
Stop patient testing until QC and strip setup are verified.

Do not allow patient results to be reported if required QC has failed.

Expected outcome: The analyzer is treated as unavailable for patient testing until acceptable QC is restored.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct Strip Type Is Being Used
steps.instructions:
Verify staff are using Roche-compatible urine test strips approved for the Urisys 1100 configuration.

Check the strip bottle label and confirm the lot matches what staff are attempting to run.

Expected outcome: Incorrect or incompatible strips are ruled out.

steps.title: Check Strip Expiration and Open-Vial Dating
steps.instructions:
Inspect the urine strip bottle for expiration date, open date, and storage condition.

Confirm the cap was closed tightly and that strips were not exposed to moisture, heat, light, or contamination.

Expected outcome: Expired, degraded, or improperly stored strips are identified before suspecting analyzer failure.

If expired or questionable strips are found, replace with a valid lot, repeat setup/QC, and stop if QC passes.

steps.title: Verify QC Material Condition
steps.instructions:
Confirm the QC material is the correct type for urine chemistry testing and has not expired.

Check open-vial stability, storage requirements, mixing requirements, and contamination risk.

Expected outcome: Failed recovery is not caused by expired, improperly stored, or mishandled QC material.

steps.title: Confirm the Correct QC Level Was Used
steps.instructions:
Verify that the QC level run matches the expected level entered or selected in the workflow.

Ensure Level 1 and Level 2 controls were not reversed, mislabeled, or poured into the wrong container.

Expected outcome: A simple QC level mix-up is ruled out.

steps.title: Check Operator Technique
steps.instructions:
Observe or review how the strip was dipped, blotted, and placed into the analyzer.

Confirm the strip pads are fully wetted, excess liquid is removed appropriately, and the strip is placed correctly without delay.

Expected outcome: QC failure is not caused by under-dipping, over-saturation, delayed placement, or poor strip handling.

steps.title: Inspect the Strip Tray and Read Area
steps.instructions:
Power down the analyzer if needed and inspect the strip tray, insert area, and visible read surface for dried urine, QC residue, dust, strip fragments, or staining.

Clean only using facility-approved methods and Roche-approved cleaning guidance.

Expected outcome: Contamination or residue that could affect pad reading is removed.

If contamination is found and cleaning restores acceptable QC, document the cleaning and stop.

steps.title: Confirm the Analyzer Completed Startup Normally
steps.instructions:
Power the Urisys 1100 off and back on according to normal user-level procedure.

Confirm it completes startup without error messages, abnormal sounds, or display issues.

Expected outcome: The analyzer is not failing basic startup checks before QC is repeated.

steps.title: Check Calibration Strip or Setup Material
steps.instructions:
If the complaint involves strip lot setup or calibration, verify the correct calibration strip or setup material is available and in usable condition.

Inspect it for damage, contamination, bending, discoloration, or incorrect storage.

Expected outcome: The lot setup process is not failing due to a damaged or contaminated setup strip.

steps.title: Verify Lot Setup Information
steps.instructions:
Confirm staff entered or loaded the correct lot information for the strip bottle being used.

Check for transposed numbers, incorrect lot selection, expired lot entry, or use of a previous bottle’s setup information.

Expected outcome: The analyzer setup matches the physical strip lot in use.

steps.title: Repeat Lot Setup Using a Known-Good Strip Lot
steps.instructions:
If available, have the lab try a different valid strip bottle or known-good lot.

Do not mix strips from different bottles.

Expected outcome: If the issue follows one strip lot, the problem is likely reagent-strip related rather than analyzer related.

steps.title: Repeat QC After Setup Is Verified
steps.instructions:
Once strips, QC material, technique, tray cleanliness, and lot setup are confirmed, repeat QC using fresh material.

Expected outcome: QC passes within the laboratory’s acceptable range.

If QC passes, return the analyzer to service per lab policy and document the corrected cause.

steps.title: Look for Pattern Across Multiple Analytes
steps.instructions:
Review whether the failure affects one chemistry pad, several pads, or all pads.

Single-pad failures often point toward strip/QC material concerns, while broad abnormal recovery may suggest technique, contamination, calibration, or analyzer read issues.

Expected outcome: The failure pattern helps determine whether the problem is external or likely internal.

steps.title: Do Not Adjust Internal Components
steps.instructions:
Do not disassemble the optics, internal reader assembly, board assemblies, or transport mechanism during frontline troubleshooting.

Expected outcome: Troubleshooting remains appropriate for Clinical Engineering field support and avoids unnecessary device damage.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported the Roche Urisys 1100 failed urine chemistry QC after setting up a new reagent strip lot.
CCR.Cause: Found expired open-vial urine strips in use and confirmed the analyzer passed QC after setup with a valid strip lot.
CCR.Resolution: Removed expired strips from use, verified correct lot setup, repeated QC successfully, and returned the Urisys 1100 to service.

helpfulDetails: Strip expiration and open date verified
Error message or failed analytes documented
Whether issue followed one lot or occurred across multiple lots

title: Roche Urisys 1800 Urine Analyzer - Rack / Sample Transport Fault
description: Troubleshooting rack movement, sample transport, strip handling, or positioning faults caused by loading, obstruction, contamination, or external setup issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Urisys 1800 is not actively processing patient samples before troubleshooting. If testing is needed immediately, have laboratory staff use another validated analyzer or follow approved downtime procedures. Expected outcome: No patient results are delayed, lost, or reported from an analyzer with an unresolved transport fault.

steps.title: Stop Additional Testing
steps.instructions: Ask staff not to load additional racks until the transport issue is understood. Confirm whether any specimens were in process when the fault occurred and whether results were completed, aborted, or need repeat testing per lab policy. Expected outcome: Questionable or incomplete urine results are not released.

steps.title: Verify the Exact Fault
steps.instructions: Review the displayed error message and ask staff when the fault occurs. Check whether the issue happens: When the rack is loaded; During rack advancement; At sample aspiration or strip handling; During startup; After cleaning or maintenance; With one rack or all racks. Expected outcome: The failure is narrowed to rack loading, rack detection, sample transport, or general mechanical movement.

steps.title: Check Rack Loading and Orientation
steps.instructions: Confirm the rack is loaded in the correct direction and seated fully in the rack input area. Verify tubes are placed correctly and are not leaning, oversized, uncapped improperly, or interfering with rack movement. Expected outcome: The rack moves normally when loaded correctly. If corrected rack placement resolves the issue, return the analyzer to service after confirming normal operation.

steps.title: Inspect the Rack for Damage or Contamination
steps.instructions: Remove the rack and inspect it for cracks, bent sections, worn guides, missing identifiers, residue, dried urine, label adhesive, or anything that could affect movement. Try a clean, known-good rack if available. Expected outcome: The analyzer transports the known-good rack without fault. If a damaged or contaminated rack caused the problem, remove that rack from use.

steps.title: Check for Obstructions in the Rack Path
steps.instructions: With the analyzer stopped and safe to access, inspect the external rack loading and transport path for: Misplaced sample tubes; Broken caps; Paper labels; Dried urine residue; Cleaning wipes; Debris; Misaligned rack guides. Do not force any mechanism or reach into moving parts. Expected outcome: The rack path is clear and unobstructed.

steps.title: Clean User-Accessible Transport Areas
steps.instructions: If residue or contamination is visible, clean only the user-accessible rack path and surrounding surfaces using facility-approved cleaning materials and the manufacturer-approved cleaning approach. Allow surfaces to dry before retesting. Expected outcome: Rack movement is no longer affected by buildup, sticky residue, or debris.

steps.title: Check Sample Tube and Label Condition
steps.instructions: Inspect tubes in the affected rack for poor label placement, loose barcode labels, excessive label thickness, caps not fully seated, or tubes that do not fit the rack properly. Replace or re-rack any questionable specimens according to lab procedure. Expected outcome: Tubes sit properly and do not interfere with rack transport or detection.

steps.title: Confirm the Analyzer Is Level and Stable
steps.instructions: Verify the Urisys 1800 is on a stable, level bench with enough clearance around the rack loading and exit areas. Make sure the analyzer has not been pushed against a wall, cable bundle, printer, waste container, or another device. Expected outcome: Rack movement is not restricted by bench setup or surrounding equipment.

steps.title: Check External Power Stability
steps.instructions: Confirm the power cord is fully seated at the analyzer and wall outlet. Verify the outlet is live and not controlled by a wall switch or loose power strip. Expected outcome: The analyzer has stable power during transport movement. Why it matters: Intermittent power or low voltage can cause incomplete movement, startup faults, or repeated transport errors.

steps.title: Restart the Analyzer If Safe
steps.instructions: If no samples are in process and lab workflow allows, power the analyzer down using the normal shutdown process. Wait briefly, then restart and allow the system to complete initialization. Expected outcome: The analyzer completes startup without rack or transport errors.

steps.title: Run a Controlled Test With a Known-Good Rack
steps.instructions: After clearing obstructions and confirming setup, have staff perform an appropriate non-patient test or QC workflow using a clean, known-good rack. Observe whether the rack advances smoothly and whether the error returns. Expected outcome: The rack transport operates normally without hesitation, grinding, repeated stopping, or error messages.

steps.title: Listen and Observe During Movement
steps.instructions: Watch for uneven movement, repeated retries, unusual motor noise, clicking, slipping, vibration, or rack skewing. Do not bypass covers, defeat safety features, or force movement. Expected outcome: Any abnormal mechanical behavior is identified without unsafe intervention.

steps.title: Review Recent Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether the issue started after: Rack change; New tube type; New barcode labels; Spill or leak; Cleaning; Analyzer relocation; Power interruption; Recent service activity. Expected outcome: A likely external trigger is identified before internal failure is suspected.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Fault Is Isolated or Repeatable
steps.instructions: If the fault occurs only with one rack or one sample setup, remove that rack or sample condition from use. If the fault occurs with multiple clean racks after obstruction, power, bench setup, and sample loading checks are complete, treat the issue as a likely internal transport fault. Expected outcome: Clinical Engineering has a clear basis for either returning the device to use or escalating for repair.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported the Roche Urisys 1800 stopped during rack advancement and displayed a sample transport fault.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found dried urine residue and a loose barcode label obstructing the rack path.
CCR.Resolution: Removed the obstruction, cleaned the user-accessible rack transport area, verified operation with a known-good rack, and returned the analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether the fault occurred during startup, loading, or sample processing
Rack ID or rack type involved
Whether one rack or all racks were affected
Sample tube type and label condition
Any visible obstruction or dried residue
Whether a known-good rack was tested
Power outlet or power cord condition
Unusual motor noise, clicking, grinding, or hesitation
Recent cleaning, spill, relocation, or service

title: Roche Urisys 1800 Urine Analyzer - Test Strip Feed Or Positioning Error
description: Troubleshooting test strip feed, strip transport, alignment, and positioning errors before removing the Urisys 1800 from service.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Urisys 1800 is not actively processing patient specimens before troubleshooting. If testing is urgent, have laboratory staff use another validated urine analyzer or approved backup testing procedure. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting does not interrupt active testing or risk questionable patient results.

steps.title: Stop Testing and Protect Result Integrity
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether a patient strip was in process when the feed or positioning error occurred. Confirm whether the test was completed, aborted, or needs to be repeated per lab policy. Expected outcome: No incomplete or unreliable urine result is released.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact symptom or message reported by staff. Check whether the issue occurs during strip loading, transport, positioning, reading, or ejection. Expected outcome: The problem is confirmed as a strip feed or positioning issue, not a general startup, optics, printer, or LIS issue.

steps.title: Inspect the Test Strip Loading Area
steps.instructions: Look for a misloaded strip, partially advanced strip, broken strip piece, dried urine, paper debris, or other obstruction in the accessible strip path. Do not force any moving parts. Expected outcome: Any visible obstruction is identified and safely removed if accessible.

steps.title: Confirm Correct Strip Type
steps.instructions: Verify the reagent strips being used are approved for the Roche Urisys 1800 configuration. Check that the strips are not expired, moisture-damaged, curled, contaminated, or from an improperly stored container. Expected outcome: The analyzer is being loaded with compatible, usable strips.

steps.title: Check Strip Orientation and Placement
steps.instructions: Confirm staff are placing strips in the correct direction and position according to the normal analyzer workflow. Incorrect placement can cause feed errors, timing faults, or failed positioning before the read cycle. Expected outcome: Strip placement is correct and repeatable.

steps.title: Check for Wet or Contaminated Strip Handling
steps.instructions: Ask whether strips are being excessively wet, dripping, or contaminated before loading. Heavy urine carryover can cause sticking, slipping, or residue buildup in the transport path. Expected outcome: Strips are blotted or handled according to lab procedure before loading.

steps.title: Inspect the Strip Transport Area for Residue
steps.instructions: With the analyzer idle and safe to access, inspect user-accessible surfaces for dried urine, crystallized residue, dust, or disinfectant buildup. Clean only external or operator-accessible areas using facility-approved procedure and materials. Expected outcome: The strip path is clean enough for normal strip movement.

steps.title: Check Waste or Strip Collection Area
steps.instructions: Verify the waste container or used-strip area is not full, misaligned, or blocking strip ejection. A backup at the end of the path can create feed or positioning faults. Expected outcome: Used strips can exit the transport path without obstruction.

steps.title: Restart the Analyzer if Appropriate
steps.instructions: If no specimen is active and staff confirm it is safe, power cycle the analyzer using the normal shutdown and startup process. Allow the system to complete startup checks without interruption. Expected outcome: Temporary control or transport state errors clear after restart.

steps.title: Run a Non-Patient Test or QC Check
steps.instructions: Have the laboratory run QC or an approved non-patient verification test after cleaning and setup checks. Observe whether the strip feeds, positions, reads, and ejects normally. Expected outcome: If the strip advances normally and QC is acceptable, return the device to service per lab policy and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Look for a Pattern
steps.instructions: Ask whether the error occurs with all strips, one strip lot, one operator, one shift, or only after cleaning. A pattern may point to strip condition, handling technique, residue, or accessory setup rather than internal failure. Expected outcome: Repeat failures are narrowed to a likely external cause or confirmed as device-related.

steps.title: Check for Abnormal Mechanical Behavior
steps.instructions: Listen for unusual grinding, repeated motor cycling, slipping, clicking, or failure to move the strip. Watch for inconsistent strip advancement or repeated misalignment. Expected outcome: Abnormal mechanical behavior is identified as a reason to escalate.

steps.title: Do Not Force the Feed Mechanism
steps.instructions: Do not push strips through the analyzer, pry at internal components, or defeat covers or sensors. Expected outcome: The device is not damaged further and staff safety is maintained.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported the Roche Urisys 1800 displayed a test strip positioning error and would not complete urine strip analysis.
CCR.Cause: Inspection found dried urine residue and a partially obstructed used-strip exit area causing inconsistent strip advancement.
CCR.Resolution: Cleaned accessible strip path surfaces, cleared the used-strip area, verified correct strip loading, and confirmed normal operation with QC before returning the analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether a patient test was active
Strip lot and expiration date checked
Visible residue, debris, or dried urine noted
Any abnormal motor noise or repeated feed cycling

title: Roche Urisys 1800 Urine Analyzer - Barcode Read Failure
description: Troubleshooting barcode read failures caused by label quality, scanner obstruction, specimen position, workflow, or external accessory issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Urisys 1800 is not actively processing patient specimens before troubleshooting the barcode issue. If patient testing must continue, have laboratory staff use an approved backup process for specimen identification and result documentation. Expected outcome: No patient result is associated with an unreadable or incorrect specimen ID.

steps.title: Verify the Barcode Complaint
steps.instructions: Ask staff what is occurring during scanning. Confirm whether the issue is: No barcode read; Intermittent reads; Wrong or incomplete ID captured; Barcode rejected by the analyzer; Only certain specimens failing; Failure after label, LIS, or workflow changes. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is understood before assuming scanner failure.

steps.title: Check Specimen Identification Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm with laboratory staff that any specimens affected by barcode failure are manually verified according to lab policy. Do not allow testing to continue if the analyzer may associate results with the wrong patient or sample. Expected outcome: Patient/specimen mismatch risk is controlled.

steps.title: Inspect the Barcode Label
steps.instructions: Check the label for wrinkles, smears, low contrast, fading, chemical contamination, torn edges, or poor print quality. Verify the barcode is not wrapped around the tube so tightly that the scanner cannot see the full code. Expected outcome: Barcode labels are readable, flat, clean, and properly positioned. If replacing or reprinting the label resolves the issue, return the analyzer to service and stop.

steps.title: Test With a Known-Good Barcode
steps.instructions: Use a specimen tube or test label that is known to scan correctly on another compatible analyzer or workstation. Expected outcome: A known-good barcode helps separate label problems from analyzer/scanner problems. If the known-good barcode reads correctly, the issue is likely label quality, barcode format, or specimen setup.

steps.title: Check Barcode Format Compatibility
steps.instructions: Confirm the barcode type being used is approved for the lab’s Urisys 1800 workflow. Ask whether the lab recently changed label printers, label templates, middleware, LIS configuration, or barcode symbology. Expected outcome: Barcode format changes are identified before hardware is suspected.

steps.title: Verify Tube and Label Placement
steps.instructions: Confirm the specimen tube is loaded in the correct orientation and seated properly in the rack or carrier. Make sure the barcode faces the scanner path and is not blocked by the rack, tube cap, overlapping label, or specimen residue. Expected outcome: The scanner has a clear line of sight to the barcode.

steps.title: Inspect the Scanner Window or Read Area
steps.instructions: Look for dust, urine residue, dried disinfectant, adhesive, label fragments, or scratches near the barcode read area. Clean only with facility-approved materials and Roche-compatible cleaning practices. Expected outcome: The scanner path is clean and unobstructed. If cleaning restores barcode reads, document the contamination and return the analyzer to service after verification.

steps.title: Check for Environmental Interference
steps.instructions: Look for strong direct light, glare, reflective labels, or nearby changes in bench lighting that could interfere with barcode reading. Reposition the analyzer or shield the scan area only if allowed by facility setup and lab workflow. Expected outcome: Barcode reading is not affected by glare or lighting conditions.

steps.title: Confirm Analyzer Startup and Error Status
steps.instructions: Check whether the Urisys 1800 completes startup normally without scanner, rack, transport, or system errors. Review any displayed barcode-related message or fault code and record the exact wording. Expected outcome: Barcode failure is not part of a broader analyzer startup or transport issue.

steps.title: Check Rack or Sample Transport Behavior
steps.instructions: Observe whether racks move smoothly and stop in the expected scan position. Listen for unusual motion, hesitation, repeated attempts, or failure to advance. Expected outcome: The barcode scanner is not being blamed for a sample positioning or transport issue.

steps.title: Restart the Analyzer if Safe
steps.instructions: If no patient testing is active and staff approve, power the analyzer down and restart it according to site procedure. After startup, test barcode reading with a known-good label. Expected outcome: A temporary software or communication hang is cleared. If barcode scanning works after restart, monitor briefly and document the recovery.

steps.title: Check External Communication Workflow
steps.instructions: If the barcode scans but is rejected, verify whether the issue is actually LIS, host, worklist, or patient ID validation related. Confirm whether the scanned ID appears on the analyzer before rejection. Expected outcome: Scanner failure is separated from data validation or middleware rejection.

steps.title: Compare Manual Entry Behavior
steps.instructions: Have authorized lab staff manually enter a specimen ID if permitted by policy. Confirm whether the analyzer accepts manual entry and proceeds normally. Expected outcome: If manual ID entry works, the issue is isolated to barcode reading, label format, scanner alignment, or scanner hardware.

steps.title: Repeat With Multiple Labels and Racks
steps.instructions: Test several known-good labels from different print batches or specimen sources. Test more than one rack or carrier if available. Expected outcome: The issue is narrowed to one label batch, rack, tube position, or the analyzer barcode system.

CCR.Complaint: Laboratory staff reported the Roche Urisys 1800 would not read specimen barcodes during sample loading.
CCR.Cause: Found multiple specimen labels wrinkled and partially blocked by the rack, preventing reliable scanner line-of-sight.
CCR.Resolution: Repositioned and reprinted affected barcode labels, verified successful reads with known-good specimens, and returned the analyzer to service.

helpfulDetails: Barcode format or label printer changes
Environmental factors such as glare or lighting changes
Indicator lights or displayed scanner status

title: Roche Urisys 1800 Urine Analyzer - Waste Tray / Strip Disposal Issue
description: Troubleshooting used-strip disposal, waste tray detection, tray overflow, strip jam, or disposal path issues on the Roche Urisys 1800.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the Urisys 1800 is not actively processing patient urine strips before removing trays, covers, or clearing the disposal area. If patient testing is urgent, have laboratory staff use another validated urine analyzer or follow approved downtime testing procedures. Expected outcome: Troubleshooting is performed without interrupting active testing or risking questionable patient results.

steps.title: Stop Testing and Confirm Result Status
steps.instructions: Ask staff whether any patient strip was in process when the waste tray or disposal issue occurred. Confirm whether the result completed, was rejected, or needs to be repeated according to lab policy. Expected outcome: No incomplete or questionable urine chemistry result is released.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Disposal Problem
steps.instructions: Ask staff what they observed: Used strip not dropping into the waste tray; Waste tray full message; Waste tray missing or not detected; Strip stuck near the transport path; Used strips backing up; Tray difficult to insert or remove; Analyzer stops during disposal; Odor, leakage, or contamination around the waste area. Expected outcome: The problem is confirmed as a strip disposal or waste tray issue, not a general transport, optics, or LIS issue.

steps.title: Inspect the Waste Tray Externally
steps.instructions: Remove the waste tray only when the analyzer is idle and safe to access. Check that the tray is fully seated, correctly oriented, and not cracked, warped, overfilled, or obstructed by stacked strips. Expected outcome: The waste tray can be removed, inspected, emptied, and reinstalled correctly. If the tray was full, misaligned, or not seated and the message clears after correction, return the analyzer to service after successful verification.

steps.title: Empty and Clean the Waste Tray
steps.instructions: Empty used strips according to laboratory biohazard waste procedure. Inspect the tray for dried urine residue, disinfectant buildup, crystallized material, or debris that could prevent proper strip drop-off or tray seating. Clean using facility-approved cleaning materials compatible with the analyzer exterior and waste components. Expected outcome: The tray is clean, dry, and able to sit flat in its normal position.

steps.title: Check for Strips Stuck Near the Disposal Area
steps.instructions: With the analyzer stopped and safe to access, visually inspect the user-accessible strip exit and disposal path. Remove any loose or partially dropped strip that can be accessed without forcing covers, tools, or internal disassembly. Expected outcome: No visible strip is blocking the disposal path. If a visible obstruction is removed and the analyzer resumes normal disposal, stop troubleshooting and document the correction.

steps.title: Verify Tray Reinstallation
steps.instructions: Reinstall the waste tray slowly and confirm it slides fully into position without resistance. Check for gaps, rocking, or interference from the bench surface, nearby objects, or waste bin liner material. Expected outcome: The tray seats normally and the analyzer no longer reports the tray as missing or full.

steps.title: Check Analyzer Position and Work Surface
steps.instructions: Confirm the Urisys 1800 is on a stable, level surface with adequate clearance around the waste tray area. A tilted analyzer, crowded bench, or object pressing against the tray can interfere with strip drop-off or tray detection. Expected outcome: The analyzer is level, stable, and the waste tray area is unobstructed.

steps.title: Inspect for Liquid Spill or Contamination
steps.instructions: Check around the waste tray opening, analyzer base, and surrounding bench for urine, disinfectant, or cleaning fluid intrusion. Look for unusual odor, sticky residue, visible corrosion, or fluid pooling. Expected outcome: No contamination is present that could interfere with tray movement or sensors. If liquid intrusion, unusual smell, or suspected internal contamination is found, remove the analyzer from service and escalate.

steps.title: Verify Strip Handling and Loading Practice
steps.instructions: Confirm staff are using the correct reagent strips and following the proper loading workflow. Improper strip placement, wet strips, curled strips, or excessive sample fluid can contribute to transport and disposal problems. Expected outcome: Strip handling is not causing strips to drag, stick, or misfeed before reaching the waste tray.

steps.title: Restart the Analyzer Only After External Checks
steps.instructions: If no obstruction or tray issue is found, power-cycle the analyzer according to normal facility procedure. Allow startup checks to complete and observe whether the waste tray or disposal message returns. Expected outcome: Temporary detection or workflow errors clear after restart.

steps.title: Run a Verification Check
steps.instructions: After the tray is emptied, cleaned, and reinstalled, have authorized lab staff run QC or an approved test process per laboratory policy. Observe strip transport through disposal. Expected outcome: The strip travels normally and drops into the waste tray without backup, jam, or warning message.

steps.title: Do Not Force the Tray or Internal Mechanism
steps.instructions: If the tray will not seat, the strip path remains blocked, or the analyzer repeatedly reports a waste tray condition after cleaning and reseating, stop troubleshooting. Forcing the tray or internal strip transport components can damage the analyzer and may create unreliable test handling. Expected outcome: The device is protected from additional damage and the issue is escalated appropriately.
